did_this_paper_use_ai_to_obtain_results_.details: The paper used a two-stage deep learning approach combining YOLOv8 for lesion segmentation and Vision Transformer (ViT) for multi-class skin cancer classification on the ISIC2019 dataset.

is_this_paper_a_review_paper_or_a_resear.details: The paper presents original experimental work involving a novel two-stage deep learning approach (YOLOv8 and Vision Transformer) for skin cancer classification. It details specific methodology, dataset usage (ISIC2019), hyperparameter settings, training procedures, and reports new quantitative results (e.g., 77.1% accuracy, F1-score of 0.76). Although published in a conference proceedings (ICHST 2025), it contains original research results rather than summarizing prior literature.

title: A Deep Learning Approach for Skin Cancer Classification Using Vision Transformer and YOLOv8 Segmentation
journal: 2025 2nd International Conference on Health Science and Technology (ICHST)
brief_summary: This study proposes a two-stage deep learning framework for automated multi-class skin cancer classification, combining YOLOv8 for lesion segmentation with a Vision Transformer (ViT) for diagnosis. The system was evaluated on the ISIC2019 dataset, which contains 25,331 images across eight distinct skin lesion categories. YOLOv8 achieved 92% segmentation accuracy to isolate affected regions, while the ViT classifier was trained for 50 epochs with specific preprocessing steps including resizing and white balance correction. The model demonstrated an overall classification accuracy of 77.1% and an F1-score of 0.76, significantly outperforming traditional machine learning baselines like SVM and KNN. The approach showed particularly strong performance for melanocytic nevus (F1-score: 0.87) and basal cell carcinoma (F1-score: 0.74). Finally, the system was implemented as a lightweight clinical decision support application named Doctor Bot, achieving an inference time of 0.07 seconds per image.

lesion_types_studied.details: One of eight distinct skin lesion categories in the ISIC2019 dataset; 867 images; F1-score 0.51.

all_task_types.target: Eight distinct skin lesion categories (Actinic Keratosis, Basal Cell Carcinoma, Benign Keratosis, Dermatofibroma, Melanoma, Melanocytic Nevus, Squamous Cell Carcinoma, Vascular Lesion)
all_task_types.details: Multi-class classification of skin lesions using a Vision Transformer (ViT) model. The model predicts discrete diagnostic categories from the ISIC2019 dataset. Performance metrics reported include accuracy (77.1%), precision (0.761), recall (0.771), and F1-score (0.76). The system also includes a YOLOv8 segmentation stage for lesion isolation, but the primary predictive task is classification.
YOLOv8-based lesion segmentation used to isolate affected skin regions prior to classification. Achieved 92% segmentation accuracy. This is a preprocessing step for the primary classification task.

datasets_used.name: ISIC2019
datasets_used.description: A public dermatological image dataset containing high-resolution dermoscopic images of skin lesions. It is also referred to as BCN20000 in the references.

use.training: Used for training the Vision Transformer (ViT) classification model and for manual annotation of 2,000 images (250 per class) for YOLOv8 segmentation training.
use.validation: Used for validation during the training of the ViT model (2,529 images).
use.testing: Used for final evaluation of the ViT classification model (2,540 images).
use.other: Used as a benchmark for comparison with traditional machine learning models (SVM, KNN, Decision Trees).

statistical_methods_used.purpose: To evaluate classification performance by calculating True Positives (TP), False Positives (FP), True Negatives (TN), and False Negatives (FN) for each class, and to analyze class bias and misclassification patterns.
To determine the optimal confidence hyperparameter (0.34) for the YOLOv8 segmentation model by finding the intersection of Precision and Recall curves to maximize both metrics.
To compare the proposed ViT model's accuracy (77.1%) against traditional machine learning baselines (SVM: 52%, KNN: 42%, DT: 40%) to demonstrate significant improvement.

hypothesis_testing.interpretation: The paper did not report any formal hypothesis testing, p-values, or significance tests. Performance comparisons with traditional machine learning baselines (SVM, KNN, DT) were made using descriptive statistics (accuracy percentages) without statistical significance testing.

ISIC2019.Notes: The ViT model was trained for 50 epochs with a batch size of 8. Training loss reached 0.004 and stabilized. Training accuracy stabilized at 99% from epoch 20. The system achieved an inference time of 0.07 seconds per image. The model showed bias toward majority classes (NV, MEL, BCC) due to dataset imbalance.

architecture_used.main_model: Two-stage approach: YOLOv8 for segmentation and Vision Transformer (ViT) for classification.
architecture_used.pretraining: ViT-B/16 model pretrained on ImageNet-21k dataset.

config.loss_function: Combined classification, regression, and segmentation losses

components: Patch Embedding: Input image split into 16x16 non-overlapping patches (196 patches for 224x224 input), flattened and linearly projected.
Positional Embedding: Added to patch embeddings to encode spatial structure.
Transformer Encoder: Consists of Multi-Head Self-Attention (MSA) layers, Multi-Layer Perceptron (MLP) blocks, Layer Normalization, and residual connections.
Classification Token: Special [CLS] token prepended to patch sequence to aggregate global contextual information.

training_details.hardware: Google Colab Pro, 51GB RAM, T4 GPU (15GB VRAM)

classification_or_output_head.output: Class probability scores for 8 skin lesion categories

approximate_parameters.Note: Swin Transformer-Small mentioned for comparison has 50 million parameters.

preprocessing_techniques: Cropping: Intelligent cropping to preserve lesion regions and remove irrelevant background areas.
Resizing: Images resized to 224x224 pixels to match ViT input requirements (YOLOv8 input was 640x640).
White Balance Correction: Color channel adjustment to neutralize white light effects and ensure consistent color representation across different imaging conditions.
Noise Reduction: Application of advanced filtering techniques to reduce image noise while preserving essential diagnostic features.
Standardization: Applied to ensure consistent input dimensions and quality.

data_augmentation_techniques.details: The paper did not report using data augmentation techniques during training. The authors explicitly stated in the Conclusion that they opted against using generative models (GANs) or data augmentation methods (such as geometric transformations, color adjustments, or class rebalancing strategies like oversampling/undersampling) due to resource limitations and concerns about synthetic data accuracy in the medical domain. They noted these as future work.

conclusions: The proposed two-stage deep learning approach combining YOLOv8 for lesion segmentation and Vision Transformer (ViT) for classification achieved an overall accuracy of 77.1% and an F1-score of 0.76 on the ISIC2019 dataset.
The model significantly outperformed traditional machine learning baselines, including Support Vector Machines (52% accuracy), K-Nearest Neighbors (42% accuracy), and Decision Trees (40% accuracy).
YOLOv8 segmentation demonstrated high precision in isolating affected skin regions, achieving a 92% segmentation accuracy with an optimal confidence threshold of 0.34.

Classification performance was heavily influenced by dataset imbalance; the model showed strong performance for majority classes such as Melanocytic Nevus (F1-score: 0.87) and Basal Cell Carcinoma (F1-score: 0.74), but lower performance for minority classes like Squamous Cell Carcinoma (F1-score: 0.45) and Actinic Keratosis (F1-score: 0.51).

The system was implemented as a lightweight clinical decision support application named 'Doctor Bot' with an inference time of 0.07 seconds per image, demonstrating potential for telemedicine and early screening.

The study acknowledges limitations regarding the lack of data augmentation and class rebalancing strategies due to resource constraints, noting that future work will address these to improve performance on underrepresented classes.
important_remarks: The study acknowledges a significant limitation: no data augmentation or class rebalancing strategies (e.g., SMOTE, GANs) were used due to resource constraints, which likely contributed to the model's bias toward majority classes like Melanocytic Nevus (50.8% of dataset) and poor performance on minority classes such as Squamous Cell Carcinoma (F1-score: 0.45) and Actinic Keratosis (F1-score: 0.51).

The model exhibits strong class imbalance bias, with performance directly correlating to dataset distribution; it achieved an F1-score of 0.87 for the majority class (Melanocytic Nevus) but struggled with underrepresented classes, indicating limited generalizability to rare pathologies without further intervention.

The research was conducted on a single public dataset (ISIC2019/BCN20000) without external validation, limiting the assessment of the model's robustness across different populations, imaging devices, or clinical settings.

No formal statistical hypothesis testing (e.g., p-values, confidence intervals) was performed to validate the significance of the performance improvements over traditional machine learning baselines (SVM, KNN, DT); comparisons were based on descriptive accuracy metrics.

The ViT model was trained for only 50 epochs with a small batch size of 8, and while training accuracy stabilized at 99%, the overall test accuracy was 77.1%, suggesting potential overfitting to the training distribution or insufficient generalization capacity given the lack of augmentation.

The segmentation stage (YOLOv8) relied on manual annotations by expert dermatologists for only 2,000 images (250 per class) from the ISIC2019 dataset, which may introduce annotator bias and limits the scalability of the segmentation component.

The system was implemented as a lightweight application ('Doctor Bot') for telemedicine, but it was tested only on standard consumer hardware (Intel Core i5-9300H, 16GB RAM, Windows 11), and its clinical utility in real-world, diverse dermatological environments remains unverified.

The paper does not report sensitivity/specificity trade-offs or confusion matrix details for all classes in a way that allows calculation of false positive/negative rates for critical cancer types (Melanoma, BCC, SCC) beyond the provided F1-scores, which may mask critical diagnostic errors.
